questions.question: What did the 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis find about online exercise compared to in-person exercise for mental health?

options.text: Online exercise is significantly inferior to in-person exercise
Online exercise was superior to no exercise (SMD = 1.08 for depression) AND non-inferior to face-to-face exercise for all tested outcomes — a game-changer for accessibility
Only in-person exercise has any mental health benefits
Digital exercise programs don't work at all

questions.explanation: A 2025 systematic review (Bhundoo, Pillay, Wilke; 18 RCTs) found online exercise was superior to no exercise for depression (SMD = 1.08), mental wellbeing (SMD = 0.79), self-efficacy (SMD = 1.1), and mood (SMD = 0.47). Critically, online was NON-INFERIOR to face-to-face for all outcomes. This removes the assumption that digital is 'second-best' and has huge implications for accessibility (rural areas, mobility limitations, social anxiety, budget constraints).

questions.question: What features make digital exercise programs effective for mental health and adherence?

options.text: Any app or website works equally well

Effective features: structured sequenced programs (clear progression), social interaction (live classes, group challenges, forums), goal-setting + progress tracking, and motivational messaging (personalized feedback)
Digital programs don't need any specific features to be effective
Only passive tracking matters; structure and social features are irrelevant

questions.explanation: Not all apps/platforms are equal. Evidence points to four key features: (1) Structured, sequenced programs (Week 1 → Week 8 progression builds self-efficacy through graduated challenges); (2) Social interaction (group exercise outperforms solo — live classes, community challenges, forums provide accountability); (3) Goal-setting + progress tracking (visualizing progress builds motivation); (4) Motivational messaging (timely reminders, personalized feedback counter low motivation). What doesn't work: purely passive tracking, generic content, no accountability.

questions.question: What are warning signs that exercise tracking has become counterproductive (hurting more than helping)?

options.text: Red flags: intense guilt/anxiety over missed workouts, obsessive checking (10+ times/day), constant comparison to others, exercising through injury/illness to 'keep the streak,' or tracking increases anxiety rather than reducing it
Everyone should track everything obsessively for best results
Guilt and anxiety about exercise are always healthy motivators

questions.explanation: The lesson lists five red flags: (1) Intense guilt/anxiety over missed workouts (shame spirals, self-punishment); (2) Obsessive checking (constantly refreshing app, checking heart rate every 5 min); (3) Constant comparison to others (leaderboard obsession, feeling inadequate); (4) Exercising through injury, illness, or burnout to maintain a 'streak'; (5) Tracking increases anxiety rather than providing useful data. If any apply, scale back or eliminate tracking — some people thrive on data, others need intuitive movement.

questions.question: According to the lesson, how should you use technology for exercise?

options.text: Technology should define your worth and control all your exercise decisions

Use technology as a tool to SUPPORT your goals (make exercise easier, more enjoyable, more sustainable) — NOT as a master that adds stress, obligation, and judgment; if it increases stress, scale back
Never use any technology for exercise tracking
Obsessively check all metrics constantly for optimal results

questions.explanation: The lesson's core principle: technology is a tool, not a master. Ask yourself: Does this tool make exercise easier, more enjoyable, or more sustainable? Or does it add stress, obligation, and judgment? If the latter, scale back or eliminate it. Recommended boundaries: limit check-ins (morning + post-workout only), turn off non-essential notifications, designate tech-free exercise days, mute leaderboards if they trigger comparison. Some people thrive on data; others need intuitive movement. Both are valid.

questions.question: What technology or tracking tools (if any) are you currently using for exercise? How do they make you feel — motivated and supported, or stressed and obligated? Is there one change you could make to improve your relationship with exercise tech?
questions.explanation: This reflection helps you assess your current tech relationship and identify needed adjustments. Healthy patterns: checking stats occasionally for useful data, feeling motivated by progress visualization, using social features for accountability without comparison. Unhealthy patterns: constant checking (anxiety-driven), guilt over missing metrics, feeling controlled by the app, comparison triggering inadequacy. Possible changes: take a 1-2 week tracking break, turn off leaderboards/comparisons, set specific check-in times only, switch to simpler tracking (pen + paper X for completed days), or eliminate tracking entirely and move intuitively.
